free_text: Fission Yields of Natural Uranium with Deuterons of 5,
10, and 13.6 MeV: Deuteron Capture and Competition
with Stripping
#doi:10.1103/RevModPhys.25.469
Data on radioactive isotopes.
The Massachusetts Institute of
Technology cyclotron.
Uranium foil 0.7 mil thick (27.8 mg/cm2) of natural
isotopic composition was the target material in all
irradiations except two early experiments at 13.6 Mev
in which natural UO3(~50 mg/cm2) was used. The target
was wrapped in an Al- foil envelope (8.5 mg/cm2) and
was secured to the water-cooled target holder by 13.7
mg/cm2 of Dural foil.
All counting was done on a set of three
intercalibrated thin-wall, cylindrical, flow Geiger
counters, 0.75 in. in diameter and 5 in. long, of a
type that has been described. The counting gas was
98.7% He and 1.3% isobutane at one atmosphere.
After irradiation, the target foil and
Al envelope were dissolved in 4M HCl and conc. HNO3.
Dilute H202 was added to convert all the U in
solution to the hexavalent state. The solution was
diluted with HCl to make a stock solution from which
aliquots were withdrawn for various chemical
operations. Nuclides were identified within a given
chemical fraction by half-period and absorption
characteristics. In no case were there significant,
deviations from literature values.

Chain yield obtained by measured cumulative yield
divided by fractional cumulative yield taken from
J.M.Alexander et al. (1957)
The error indicated is simply the mean
deviation of replicate experiments and is not
intended to represent the errors of the experiment.
On an absolute basis the cross sections
for producing each nuclide are probably no better than
25%. The largest fraction of this error is attributed
to uncertainties in the beam intensity.

Counted equilibrium mixture.
In thermal fission of 235U the mass-129 chain
yield should be taken as twice the 129Sb yield. The
yields in in this table are for 129Sb.

conversion electrons pf 105mRh.

of Al to absorb radiation of 103Ru and 106Ru.
Mass assignment is not certain, not observed in
thermal fission but found in 14-MeV neutron fission
of 235U.
Measured 132I in equilibrium with 77-hr 132Te;
yield is that of parent.
Measured 143Pr in separate cerium fraction in
which 33-hr 143Ce had decayed; yield is that of parent.
Counted equilibrium mixture though 70 mg/cm2 of
Al to absorb radiations of 141Ce and 144Ce.  Upper
limit, contribution of long-lived impurities possible.

DATA-ERR in COMMON changed to ERR-SYS and
moved to BIB section as upper limit
On. DECAY-DATA field moved.
Subtraction of the masses of complementary products
(i.e., those of the same yield) from the assumed mass
of the fissioning nucleus.
Authors indicate that the fissioning nucleus has to
be taken to be mass 239, 240, and 240 for deuteron
energies of 5, 10, and 13.6 MeV, respectively. The
stable isotopes of U are 234, 235 and 238.
Consequently, we deal with 238U as a target nucleus.
The error in nu bar has been estimated in
the following way: If an error in the yield of a
light product is assigned, this can be converted to
an error in mass by assuming the smooth curves to be
the best fit to the data. The error in the mass of
the heavy complementary product is obtained by
determining the range of heavy masses corresponding
to the range in yield of the light mass. The error in
s is taken to be the root-mean-square error in the
masses of light and heavy products.
The error in relative yields has been
assumed to be 10% for masses 89 and 105 and 20% for
masses 72, 77, and 83.
Obtained by extrapolating of smooth mass-
distribution curve.
